label: tetlock-est-definition-public-record
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: estimate ONE sub-question in isolation. You see only the facts and
this sub-question; you must not analyze the broader dispute. Give a calibrated
probability and the few considerations doing the real work.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

## Sub-question to estimate (in isolation)

**Do the Signal group messages fall within San Jose v. Superior Court's definition of 'public records' despite being created by a bidder's consultant, exchanged on personal devices, and largely auto-deleted?**

What would have to be true: San Jose's content/context/purpose test would need to be satisfied — i.e., the substance (franchise terms, diversion targets, RFP timing, 'what council will accept') would need to relate to the conduct of public business, not merely personal/political chatter — notwithstanding that a private consultant created the channel and non-officials (GreenCycle execs, advocates) participated; and the one-week disappearing-message setting would need to be treated as bearing on later spoliation/retention, not on whether the writings were 'prepared, owned, used, or retained' at the moment of creation.
text: {"probability":82,"key_considerations":["Content is the dominant San Jose factor here, and it cuts strongly toward 'public record' status: franchise terms, diversion targets, RFP timing, and 'what council will realistically accept' are squarely about the conduct of public business (negotiation/award of an $180M franchise), not personal or campaign chatter — this is arguably a stronger fact pattern than San Jose itself.","San Jose holds that writings on personal devices/accounts are 'retained by' the agency (Gov. Code former §6252(e)/current §7920.530-ish; verify current codification) when a public official prepares, sends, receives, or uses them in connection with public business — so the three councilmembers' own participation (sending, receiving, and substantively engaging) likely satisfies the test even though the channel was created and hosted by a private consultant on a third-party platform.","A private consultant/bidder creating the Signal group and non-official participants (GreenCycle execs, advocates) being present does not, by itself, defeat classification — San Jose's inquiry focuses on the individual official's relationship to the writing (did the official prepare/receive/use it re: public business), not on who controls the platform; but messages exchanged solely among non-officials with no councilmember involvement are on weaker footing since San Jose's test applies to writings of public officials/employees.","The one-week auto-delete setting is best treated as a retention/spoliation issue arising after the fact, not as negating public-record status at creation — 'public record' classification attaches at the moment a qualifying writing is prepared, sent, or received in connection with public business, independent of whether it later survives; this tracks the sub-question's framing and is consistent with how courts have treated destroyed/lost records in CPRA enforcement (the records existed as public records even if now irretrievable).","Public-record status is a lower bar than disclosability: even if some messages touch on deliberative or politically sensitive strategizing ('what council will accept'), that affects exemption analysis (deliberative-process privilege, §7922.000/6255 balancing — verify current section numbers post-2021 recodification) rather than whether the writing qualifies as a 'public record' in the first instance, making the classification question easier to resolve in the affirmative than the ultimate disclosure fight."],"what_evidence_would_change_this":"Probability would rise toward the high 80s/90s if the surviving 40 screenshots show councilmembers actively drafting, editing, or directing franchise terms/RFP strategy (strong 'use' by officials). It would drop meaningfully (toward 50-60%) if forensic/testimonial evidence showed the three councilmembers were largely passive/silent observers who never authored or responded to substantive messages (weakening the 'prepared, owned, used, or retained by' officials element for their portion of the exchange), or if a reviewing court applied a narrower reading limiting San Jose to writings substantially authored by the official rather than merely received in a group chat controlled by a private party. Any subsequent CA appellate decision narrowing San Jose's application to third-party-hosted group chats with non-official participants would also be directly relevant and should be checked for."}

key_considerations: Content is the dominant San Jose factor here, and it cuts strongly toward 'public record' status: franchise terms, diversion targets, RFP timing, and 'what council will realistically accept' are squarely about the conduct of public business (negotiation/award of an $180M franchise), not personal or campaign chatter — this is arguably a stronger fact pattern than San Jose itself.

San Jose holds that writings on personal devices/accounts are 'retained by' the agency (Gov. Code former §6252(e)/current §7920.530-ish; verify current codification) when a public official prepares, sends, receives, or uses them in connection with public business — so the three councilmembers' own participation (sending, receiving, and substantively engaging) likely satisfies the test even though the channel was created and hosted by a private consultant on a third-party platform.

A private consultant/bidder creating the Signal group and non-official participants (GreenCycle execs, advocates) being present does not, by itself, defeat classification — San Jose's inquiry focuses on the individual official's relationship to the writing (did the official prepare/receive/use it re: public business), not on who controls the platform; but messages exchanged solely among non-officials with no councilmember involvement are on weaker footing since San Jose's test applies to writings of public officials/employees.

The one-week auto-delete setting is best treated as a retention/spoliation issue arising after the fact, not as negating public-record status at creation — 'public record' classification attaches at the moment a qualifying writing is prepared, sent, or received in connection with public business, independent of whether it later survives; this tracks the sub-question's framing and is consistent with how courts have treated destroyed/lost records in CPRA enforcement (the records existed as public records even if now irretrievable).

Public-record status is a lower bar than disclosability: even if some messages touch on deliberative or politically sensitive strategizing ('what council will accept'), that affects exemption analysis (deliberative-process privilege, §7922.000/6255 balancing — verify current section numbers post-2021 recodification) rather than whether the writing qualifies as a 'public record' in the first instance, making the classification question easier to resolve in the affirmative than the ultimate disclosure fight.
data.what_evidence_would_change_this: Probability would rise toward the high 80s/90s if the surviving 40 screenshots show councilmembers actively drafting, editing, or directing franchise terms/RFP strategy (strong 'use' by officials). It would drop meaningfully (toward 50-60%) if forensic/testimonial evidence showed the three councilmembers were largely passive/silent observers who never authored or responded to substantive messages (weakening the 'prepared, owned, used, or retained by' officials element for their portion of the exchange), or if a reviewing court applied a narrower reading limiting San Jose to writings substantially authored by the official rather than merely received in a group chat controlled by a private party. Any subsequent CA appellate decision narrowing San Jose's application to third-party-hosted group chats with non-official participants would also be directly relevant and should be checked for.
